description: Diagnose video game lag, stutter and hitching on oliver-win. Focuses on the things that actually cause frame drops: GPU/CPU saturation, thermal & power throttling, DPC/interrupt latency, memory paging, disk stalls and network loss.
title: Gaming — Lag & Stutter Diagnosis (oliver-win)

options.content:
## How to read this dashboard

Go **top to bottom** — the goal is to find *what is limiting your frames* right now.

1. **Bottleneck verdict** (top-right): tells you if you're **GPU-bound**, **CPU-bound**, or **neither** (lag is elsewhere — network/disk).
2. **GPU-bound** → look at the **GPU** row. ~100% util is normal & good. If you want more FPS, lower settings. Check *Time spent throttled* — any sustained bars mean the card is being held back (usually power or heat).
3. **CPU-bound** → look at the **Per-core (unstacked)** panel. One line pinned at ~100% while others are low = a single main thread is the wall. Lowering graphics settings will NOT help; reduce CPU-side load (AI traffic, sim/object density) or you need a faster-per-core CPU.
4. **Neither maxed but still laggy** → check **Network** (discards/errors = online rubber-banding) and **Disk** (latency/queue spikes = asset-streaming hitches), and **Memory** (pagefile activity = hard stutters).

**Rule of thumb:** *stacked* totals can look scary; what matters is whether any **single** resource (one GPU, one CPU core, one disk) is actually maxed. This dashboard is built so the unstacked panels make that obvious.

panels.description:
Automatic call: compares GPU utilization against the busiest single CPU core.

- GPU-BOUND: GPU >= 90% and no single core maxed. Frames limited by the graphics card — lower settings for more FPS.
- CPU-BOUND: a single core >= 90% while GPU has headroom. One game thread is the wall — graphics settings won't help.
- BOTH HOT: GPU high AND a core high — balanced/heavy load.
- NEITHER: nothing maxed. If you still feel lag it's network, disk or paging (see rows below), or a frame-rate cap.

1.text: GPU-BOUND — lower settings for more FPS

2.text: CPU-BOUND — one core is the wall

panels.title: Bottleneck verdict (live)
Verdict — is something throttling or starving right now?

panels.description: GPU thermal or power throttling is active. ANY of these going to 1 means the GPU is being forced to slow down — the #1 cause of mid-game framerate drops. Sustained > brief blips is what hurts.
panels.title: GPU throttling NOW

panels.description: Processor queue length: number of threads waiting for a CPU core. Sustained > 0 (rule of thumb > number-of-cores) means the CPU can't keep up and frames are being delayed — CPU-bound stutter.
panels.title: CPU run-queue (threads waiting)

panels.description: Free RAM. When this approaches zero Windows starts paging to disk and you get hard hitches as data is swapped. Watch for it diving during a level load.
panels.title: RAM available

panels.description: GPU temperature. As it climbs toward the throttle limit (typically ~83-88°C) you'll start seeing the throttling indicators trip.
panels.title: GPU temperature

panels.description: GPU core, memory-controller, encoder and decoder utilization. ~100% GPU util means you're GPU-bound (expected at high settings). If GPU util is LOW while you still have lag, the bottleneck is CPU/network — look at the other rows.
panels.title: GPU utilization

panels.description: Time per scrape interval spent throttled, broken down by cause. Any non-zero rate here directly explains frame drops. SW power cap = hitting the power limit; thermal = too hot; HW power brake = transient over-current.
panels.title: Time spent throttled (by cause)

panels.description: GPU core clock vs its max. When throttling kicks in the actual clock drops well below max — visible proof of lost performance.
panels.title: GPU clock vs max

panels.description: Power draw vs the enforced limit, and NVENC encoder latency. For streamed/remote play (Moonlight, GeForce NOW, OBS) rising encoder latency is felt directly as input lag. Power pinned at the limit explains SW power cap throttling.
panels.title: GPU power & encoder latency

panels.description: VRAM used vs total. Running out of VRAM forces textures to spill into system RAM over PCIe — a major source of texture pop-in and stutter in modern titles.
panels.title: VRAM usage

panels.description: PCIe link width and generation actually negotiated. If this drops below the GPU's max (e.g. x16 → x8, or Gen4 → Gen1 at idle is normal, but Gen1/x8 under load is a problem) it throttles the CPU↔GPU bandwidth and causes stutter, especially when VRAM spills.
panels.title: PCIe link (width & gen)

panels.description: Total CPU busy (100% − idle). A single thread maxed out won't show here but the run-queue panel will. Sustained high total + high run-queue = CPU-bound.
panels.title: CPU total utilization

panels.description: Time spent in DPCs and hardware interrupts. High/spiky DPC or interrupt time is the classic cause of Windows micro-stutter and audio crackle even when overall CPU usage looks fine — usually a driver (network, GPU, USB, audio) misbehaving.
panels.title: DPC & interrupt time (stutter source)

panels.description: Each line is ONE core's busy %, drawn 0-100% and NOT stacked. Look for a single line pinned near 100% while the others sit lower — that is a CPU main-thread bottleneck (common in flight/strategy sims). If every line is moderate (none maxed) the CPU is NOT your wall, even if 'total busy' looks high. Hover to see which core number is the hot one.
panels.title: Per-core utilization — UNSTACKED (find the pegged main thread)

panels.description: Available physical RAM and committed (promised) bytes vs the commit limit. When committed approaches the limit, or available nears zero, Windows pages to disk → hard hitches.
panels.title: Memory available & committed

panels.description: Pages read/written to the pagefile per second. This is the smoking gun for paging-induced stutter: any sustained activity here means the game is being swapped to disk mid-session.
panels.title: Pagefile activity (hard paging)

panels.description: Throughput on each NIC. A saturated uplink (e.g. a large download running) shares the pipe with your game traffic and inflates ping/jitter. Watch the active wired adapter.
panels.title: Network throughput

panels.description: Discarded/errored packets and output queue length. ANY sustained discards or errors, or a non-zero output queue, means packet loss / congestion at the NIC — felt as rubber-banding and hit-registration problems in online games.
panels.title: Packet loss / errors / queue

panels.description: Average latency per I/O on each volume (latency-seconds ÷ ops). Spikes here cause traversal stutter and texture/shader pop-in as the game streams assets from disk. The drive holding your games is the one to watch.
panels.title: Disk latency per I/O

panels.description: Outstanding I/O requests queued per volume. A persistently deep queue means the disk can't keep up with asset streaming — sustained loading hitches.
panels.title: Disk queue depth
